---
title: "Research"
toc: false
page-layout: article
css: styles.css
---

## Forthcoming

<div class="paper-entry">
<p class="paper-title"><a class="abstract-toggle" data-id="abs-1">Machine Learning for Detecting Collusion and Capacity Withholding in Wholesale Electricity Markets</a></p>
<p class="paper-info">Forthcoming with <a href="https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=c52V71kAAAAJ&hl=en">Martin Huber</a> and Jeremy Proz</p>
<div class="paper-abstract-wrap" id="abs-1">
<p class="abstract-body">Collusion and capacity withholding in electricity wholesale markets are important mechanisms of market manipulation. This study applies a refined machine learning–based cartel-detection algorithm to two cartel cases in the Italian electricity market and evaluates its out-of-sample performance. We propose novel screens capturing the capacity-withholding behavior of electricity providers and find that including such screens—derived from the day-ahead spot market rather than the balancing market—can improve cartel detection. Overall, the study provides a proof of concept for the strong potential of supervised machine learning techniques to detect and dismantle cartels in electricity markets.</p>
</div>
<p class="paper-links">
<a href="files/paper_energy_compressed.pdf"><i class="bi bi-file-earmark-pdf"></i> PDF</a>
<a href="#"><i class="bi bi-easel2"></i> Slides</a>
</p>
</div>

<div class="paper-entry">
<p class="paper-title"><a class="abstract-toggle" data-id="abs-2">The Spatial Structure of Competitive Interactions: Evidence from Public Procurement Networks in Quebec</a></p>
<p class="paper-info">Working paper with <a href="https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=cSCDeLEAAAAJ&hl=fr">Julie LeGallo</a> and <a href="https://scholar.google.fr/citations?user=UpPlMYwAAAAJ&hl=fr">Jean Dubé</a></p>
<div class="paper-abstract-wrap" id="abs-2">
<p class="abstract-body">How do local markets form spatially? Using bid-level data from Quebec's municipal paving sector (2012–2019), we construct a geographical bidder overlap (GBO) index measuring supply-side substitutability between postal codes. Gravity-style regressions show that GBO decays with distance (elasticity ≈ −1) and increases with location similarity. Community detection on the GBO network identifies 47 functional market areas that diverge substantially from administrative boundaries. Exponential Random Graph models indicate that transitivity, geographic proximity, and agglomeration are each associated with market integration. The 47 functional market areas diverge substantially from administrative boundaries, suggesting that standard regional units are imperfect proxies for competitive geography in this sector.</p>
</div>
<p class="paper-links">
<a href="files/paper_1.pdf"><i class="bi bi-file-earmark-pdf"></i> PDF</a>
<a href="#"><i class="bi bi-easel2"></i> Slides</a>
</p>
</div>


## Work in Progress

<div class="paper-entry">
<p class="paper-title"><a class="abstract-toggle" data-id="abs-3">Business as Usual: The Case of the Antitrust Inquiry in Quebec, Canada</a></p>
<p class="paper-info">Job Market Paper</p>
<div class="paper-abstract-wrap" id="abs-3">
<p class="abstract-body">This paper analyzes the dynamic impact of antitrust intervention on competition outcomes in public procurement markets. Leveraging the largest antitrust inquiry in Quebec between 2011 and 2014, I implement a panel event-study analysis and a difference-in-differences approach to identify the effects of antitrust enforcement on different levels of procurement outcomes. I rely on an entropy covariate strategy to improve the balance between treated and untreated municipalities, and combine matching and panel fixed-effects methods to uncover the effects of policy intervention. I estimate the average treatment effect and then disaggregate the effect over time after the intervention to understand the dynamic effects of the intervention. I also investigate the heterogeneity of treatment effects for relevant players in the market, notably the case of cartel-related and regional level firms. While I am unable to identify lasting effects on the procurement market for the bid-rigging case at hand, I show that antitrust intervention significantly alters the strategy of key players in the market.</p>
</div>
<p class="paper-links">
<a href="#"><i class="bi bi-file-earmark-pdf"></i> PDF</a>
<a href="files/paper2_slides_jdd.pdf"><i class="bi bi-easel2"></i> Slides</a>
<a href="https://github.com/iamarin"><i class="bi bi-github"></i> Code</a>
</p>
</div>

<script>
document.querySelectorAll('.abstract-toggle').forEach(function(title) {
  title.addEventListener('click', function(e) {
    e.preventDefault();
    var wrap = document.getElementById(this.dataset.id);
    wrap.classList.toggle('expanded');
  });
});
</script>
